intro: Porting from Java to Kotlin modernizes a codebase within the same JVM ecosystem, maintaining full interoperability while drastically reducing boilerplate code. This conversion targets safer engineering defaults by eliminating whole classes of common exceptions like null pointer issues directly at the compiler level. The result is a cleaner, more expressive architecture with modern features like coroutines.

diffs.from: Statically typed with primitive vs object divisions and implicit nullability permissions for all reference values.
diffs.to: Statically typed with clear nullability boundaries baked directly into type declarations and unified object types.

diffs.from: Verbose syntax requiring semicolons, explicit getter/setter boilerplate, and rigid class declarations.
diffs.to: Concise, expressive layout removing semicolons, auto-generating properties, and providing functional language extensions.

diffs.from: Standard JVM runtime memory layouts allocating classes and garbage collecting object states.
diffs.to: Identical JVM runtime behavior with extra optimizations like inline functions preventing object overhead in lambdas.

diffs.from: Native OS threads, platform thread pools, or newly introduced project Loom virtual thread mechanisms.
diffs.to: Lightweight, compiler-driven coroutines that pause execution state without blocking underlying runtime threads.

diffs.from: Traditional core Java packages expanded carefully across major version releases over decades.
diffs.to: JDK enhancements focusing on functional collections, sequence streams, and utilities optimized for shorter code expressions.

examples.title: Data Class Definition
examples.description: Replacing standard Java POJO structures with concise Kotlin data classes.

examples.title: Null-Safe Operations
examples.description: Converting explicit null checking structures into safe-call chains.

tips.title: Leverage Interoperability
tips.body: Convert your codebase safely page by page. Kotlin communicates flawlessly with existing Java files inside the same build loop.

tips.title: Replace Utilities with Extensions
tips.body: Eliminate static utility classes (e.g., StringUtils). Refactor them into clean Kotlin extension functions attached to target classes.

tips.title: Enforce Strict Nullability
tips.body: Audit your Java parameters before porting; map them consciously to nullable (?) or non-nullable types based on actual code constraints.

tips.title: Simplify Collections Management
tips.body: Switch standard streams definitions to Kotlin's direct collection functions like map, filter, and associateBy for cleaner execution syntax.

faqs.question: Does Kotlin replace Lombok?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kotlin natively provides properties, data classes, and constructors, rendering Lombok annotations unnecessary.

faqs.question: Are Kotlin data classes identical to Java records?
faqs.answer: They are conceptually very similar. Both generate structural equality and getters, but Kotlin data classes allow class mutability and handle inheritance differently.

faqs.question: Is there a performance cost when moving to Kotlin?
faqs.answer: No, Kotlin compiles directly down to JVM bytecode, making its raw execution speed equivalent to native Java code.
